annotations.content: When I was young I really saw myself as being an artist, a musician... I never foresaw my life as being connected with the environment or certainly with enegy issues, I didn't even saw myself as becoming a writer.

I read a book called 'Limits to Growth', it was published in 1972, I was 21 at the time. And this was the first real dawning awareness that I was living in a society that was fundamentally unsustainable.

When I read the book suddenly all the other things I was doing, music and art, just seemed to recede in importance. In 'Limits to Growth' computer modelling studies has shown that in the most likely instance industrial society would more or less self-destruct in the early part of the 21st century. Even the possibility of human extinction was something that was thinkable given the trends that were happening with growing human population and depleating resources. It was clear even then that we were on a trajectory that would not end well.

Well this is our ancester wall. The light isn't too good in here. This is a great uncle who was a baseball player. This is childhood pictures of me, childhood pictures of Janet. Janet and I are only children and all of our parents are gone and we have inherited boxes and boxes of photos. And we are sort of the end of the line so we have no one to give all of these photos to... it will all just end up in the dumpster somewhere.
I'll just let you have it.

Let's just look at oil. We started in 1850 using 0 barels of oil per year. And that gradually increased and increased to a hundred million barrels of oil per day. So that is an extraordinary rate of increase  and what has economic growth done during this time, it's basically done the same thing. And the same thing with human population. So the question is, where do we go from here? Can we continue to support 7.5 billion and of course that number's going to grow, is it going to 8,9,10,11 billion? What's gonna happen to economic growth? If that line we started with doesn't continue growing? We only have so much time? How fast do we need to be making this transition? Well it turns out, at least 10 times faster than we currently are. And the research, yes, it's going into energy storage, it's going into solar and wind, but we don't have nearly as much research going on as we need into how we're going to transition the entire transport sector, the entire food system, the entire manufacturing sector...
I know it on an intellectual level, most days. And some days I know it on an emotional level. Could you draw me a picture of what this does to you?

It makes me profoundly question the whole basis of human existence in the 21st century... And it makes me lose a lot of sleep... Because the... you know most people don't get this. If 7.5 billion of us want to have just a nice house and not a big car but a functional car to get around and a refrigerator... and a couple of children... That doesn't sound like much! And yet we are destroying the planet and future generations in order to get it, how does that happen? But it's happening....
Yea... the result is gonna be an immense human tragedy.
Is that why you don't have children?
That is the reason we don't have children, yes.
